meta.title: macro, mega

source.publication: Section 4: Roots, Prefixes, Suffixes and Foreign Words (owner-supplied reference)
source.usage_note: Personal study only. Root, words and meanings transcribed faithfully from the source PDF; a small number of well-attested real words on the same root are included where the source list was too short to form a complete family (4 to 8 members). Parts, glosses, context sentences, mentor note, attempt and construct options are the teaching layer, authored for the Language Garden (LANGUAGE_GARDEN_BIBLE §10).

meta.reviewer_notes: Batch 2: transcribed from the owner's roots (Greek and Latin) reference; 3 taught, 2 reach.

root.label: macro, mega
root.mentor_note: macro and mega both point the same direction: bigger, and often visibly so.

attempt.direction_prompt: Visible only under a microscope, or visible without one?

context_sentences: Even without the microscope, the tumour's macroscopic edges were already visible to the surgeon.
The geologist trusted macroscopic clues, the cracks and colour bands any visitor could see, more than a lab result that took a week.
The marshal raised her megaphone and the entire platform fell quiet at once.
Without a megaphone, the coach's instructions never reached the runners at the far end of the track.
Visitors still gather at dawn to watch the sun rise directly behind the site’s tallest megalith.
No one has fully explained how the builders moved a single megalith weighing forty tonnes across open country.
Driving in from the airport, she watched three once separate towns blur into a single sprawling megalopolis.
Planners now treat the region as one megalopolis, sharing water, transport and power across old city lines.

construct_options.text: A vast urban region formed when several cities grow together into one
A single building so large it could house an entire city’s population
A city especially known for its ancient stone monuments

context_sentences: The processor's clock speed, once measured in a few hundred megahertz, now runs a thousand times faster.
Two hundred megahertz was considered remarkable in a home computer only a few decades ago.

construct_options.text: A unit for measuring a very high frequency, equal to a million hertz
A unit for measuring the physical size of a computer
A unit for measuring an extremely small distance, a millionth of a metre
